title: Lemma 2.1: reduced-word orthogonality identities (heart of boundary-piece computation)

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**Location:** Proof of Lemma 2.1 (the intersection lemma $\iota^\sharp(e_1)q_2\mathcal K = \overline{\iota^\sharp(e_1MJMJe_2)\mathcal K}\subset q\mathcal K$).

The proof rests on three word-orthogonality identities that are justified by a single sentence:

1. $(e_1-e)(M\ominus M_1)J(M\ominus M_1)Je_2 = 0$, equivalently $e_2(M\ominus M_1)J(M\ominus M_1)J(e_1-e)=0$.
2. $(e_1-e)J(M\ominus M_1)Je_2 = 0$.
3. $(e_1-e)(M\ominus M_1)e_2 = 0$.

The stated reason ("a vector in $(M\ominus M_1)J(M\ominus M_1)J(e_1-e)L^2M$ is spanned by $\mathcal H_{v_1\cdots v_n}$ with $v_j\notin\mathcal V(\mathcal G_2)$ for some $j$, hence orthogonal to $L^2M_2$") is an assertion, not a proof.

**What must be proved (rigorously, in the reduced-word framework $L^2M=\bigoplus_w\mathcal H_w$ of [CaFi17]):**
- The precise action of left multiplication by $a\in M\ominus M_1$, right multiplication $JbJ$ ($b\in M\ominus M_1$), and the projections $e_1,e_2,e$ on basis vectors $\xi_w$ (first-letter / last-letter bookkeeping).
- Why $(e_1-e)L^2M$ is exactly the span of words supported on $\mathcal V(\mathcal G_1)$ but **not** on $\mathcal V(\mathcal G_1\cap\mathcal G_2)$.
- Why applying $(M\ominus M_1)J(M\ominus M_1)J$ (resp. one-sided versions) to such a vector necessarily yields words containing a letter outside $\mathcal V(\mathcal G_2)$, hence orthogonal to $e_2L^2M = L^2M_2$.

Additionally, the claims "Similarly, we have $M_1e_1J(M\ominus M_1)Je_2,\ JM_1Je_1(M\ominus M_1)e_2\subset\iota^\sharp(MJMJe)\mathcal K$" are stated with no argument at all and must be written out.

This is the single most load-bearing computation in the paper; a superficial argument risks dropping cross terms.
